question.text: A company enters into a 3×9 FRA at a fixed rate of 7%. After 3 months, the prevailing reference rate for the 6-month period is 8.5%. What is the nature of the settlement?

answer.text: The seller of the FRA pays the difference to the company, as the reference rate exceeds the agreed fixed rate
The company pays the difference to the seller, as the reference rate exceeds the agreed fixed rate
No settlement is required as both rates are above 5%
The company receives the full notional principal from the seller

explanation.text: **The Logic:** In a 3×9 FRA at 7%, the company has locked in a borrowing rate of 7%. When the reference rate (8.5%) is higher than the agreed rate (7%), the seller compensates the company for the difference (1.5% on the notional principal, present-valued). This protects the company against the interest rate rise it was hedging. **The Trap:** The company is the buyer (hedger) — when rates go up, the seller pays the difference to the buyer. No principal is exchanged in FRAs; settlement is based on the interest rate differential. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** FRA notation '3×9' means the contract starts after 3 months and covers the period from month 3 to month 9 (a 6-month period). The settlement is typically on a present value basis.
